Patch Set 1:

Given that we are trying to remove the cfs_ wrappers and use native Linux APIs, why not just change the callers to ktrhread_run() directly?  This patch is already changing all of the callers. 

Also, in some places there could be more tab conversion.

Patch Set 2:

Hello!

libcfs/linux/linux-prim.c: shouldnt export unshare_fs_struct unconditionaly. It will collides with modern kernels.

Patch Set 2:

It is protected by #ifndef HAVE_UNSHARE_FS_STRUCT. So modern kernels aren't impacted.

Patch Set 2:

Tests received by maloo, run on el6/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/abeceda0-8c9a-11e2-af77-52540035b04c).  Ran 2 tests.  1 tests failed: lustre-initialization-1.

Patch Set 2: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(1 inline comment)

See comments

Patch Set 2: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(1 inline comment)

Have you reviewed the signal handling semantics? I have a similar patch started and it's stalled on sorting out the proper signal handling. kernel_thread() inherits the process signal handling and blocks them -- so that unblocking them will allow them to be caught. kthread_run's kthreadd context ignores all signals, so unblocking them just causes them to fall through and be ignored. Anything expecting signals won't work properly in that case.

Patch Set 2: (2 inline comments)

Jeff,

First thanks a lot for reviewing the patch.

As for signal handling semantics difference between kthread_run and kernel_thread(), as I audited all kernel_thread() callers in Lustre, they all call daemonize() the first thing in the child thread. Since daemonize() takes care of blocking and flushing all signals, all child threads will have all signal blocked after calling daemonize().

OTOH, it seems that Lustre never unlocks signals since I don't see any caller of sigdelsetmask() or sigprocmask() in Lustre kernel code. The only signal manipulation places (cfs_block_xxx functions) call sigaddsetmask to increase blocked signal coverage. cfs_restore_sigs() might be an exception, but the sigset passed in is always fetched from current thread, so it doesn't unblock any signals for daemonized()ed threads. So I don't think Lustre ever unblocks signals in daemonized kernel threads.

Therefore, I think we can just achieve the same effect by replacing kernel_thread()+daemonize() with kthread_run().

Does above explanation make sense to you?

Thanks.

Patch Set 2:

And maloo failed because of the symbol export problem pointed by Alexey Shvetsov. I will fix it once we settle on kthread_run replacement issue.

Patch Set 2:

Tests received by maloo, run on el6/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/41a17ba0-8d2b-11e2-abc6-52540035b04c).  Ran 2 tests.  1 tests failed: lustre-initialization-1.

Patch Set 2:

Tao, you are correct - ptlrpc_daemonize() is only used for service threads, and they are never accepting signals from userspace.

FYI, virtually all waiting in Lustre is done via l_wait_event(), which is in charge of unblocking signals after there has been a chance for RPCs to time out.

parents.subject: LU-2901 mdt: duplicate link names in directory

commit.subject: LU-2850 kernel: 3.8 upstream kills daemonize()
commit.message:
LU-2850 kernel: 3.8 upstream kills daemonize()

kernel_thread() is a low level kernel function.
Instead of calling kernel_thread() and relying on daemonize()
to create new kernel threads, we should really switch to
kthread_run() and elimilate daemonize().

All callers are converted except for those in
lnet/klnds/gnilnd/gnilnd*, as requested by James.

Signed-off-by: Peng Tao <tao.peng@emc.com>
Signed-off-by: James Simmons <uja.ornl@gmail.com>
Change-Id: I9f977bfc5d58192957d8c8d634204f4910edfa2a
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/5655
Tested-by: Hudson
Tested-by: Maloo <whamcloud.maloo@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Keith Mannthey <keith.mannthey@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Nathaniel Clark <nathaniel.l.clark@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
